sprintGoal: Close the structural gaps between MemClaw's current 52% Loop Engineering support and 100% enablement. The Loop Engineering framework defines 5 load-bearing ideas: (1) Layer 4 Substrate, (2) the 5-Move cycle, (3) Isolated Verification, (4) Turn-Distance Cost, (5) Faithful Amplifier of Judgment. MemClaw already covers Layer 4, most of the 5 moves, and the amplifier well — the three structural gaps are: Isolated Verification (10% supported — no sandboxed verification harness), Turn-Distance Cost (30% — no turn indexing or TDCF exposure), and Move 5 Propulsion Scheduling (60% — no proactive loop re-firing). This sprint builds: (LE-01) a verification_runs storage table + service for recording isolated verification outcomes; (LE-02) memclaw_verify_run — an MCP tool that executes eval commands in an isolated sub-agent and records success/failure against procedure reliability; (LE-03) turn-index tracking on writes and the Turn-Distance Cost Factor (TDCF) computation; (LE-04) memclaw_cost_audit — exposes TDCF and cheapest-alternative analysis for any recall or write decision; (LE-05) memclaw_schedule_loop — proactive propulsion tickets + a worker loop engine driven by lifecycle cron, closing the scheduling half of Move 5.

workItems.objective: Add storage foundation for the Isolated Verification subsystem. New alembic migration creates verification_runs table (tenant_id, memory_id, procedure_id, command, command_type, outcome, details, triggered_at, turn_distance), FK to memories ON DELETE CASCADE. New service loop_engineering/verification_store.py exposes insert + query (by memory_id, by procedure_id, latest N). No new columns on the existing memories table — turn_index is a caller-managed annotation stored in verification_runs context.
acceptanceCriteria: New alembic migration creates verification_runs table with columns: id (uuid PK, server_default gen_random_uuid()), tenant_id (uuid NOT NULL, FK tenants.id ON DELETE CASCADE), memory_id (uuid NULLABLE, FK memories.id ON DELETE CASCADE — NULL when a procedure is verified independently of a specific memory), procedure_id (uuid NULLABLE — NULL for memory-only verification), command (text NOT NULL), command_type (enum/varchar NOT NULL: bash|http|filesystem), outcome (varchar NOT NULL: pass|fail|error), details (jsonb DEFAULT {}), triggered_at (timestamptz NOT NULL server_default now()), turn_distance (integer NULL — NULL means N/A, not computed); composite index on (tenant_id, memory_id, triggered_at DESC); composite index on (tenant_id, procedure_id, triggered_at DESC)

core-api/src/core_api/services/loop_engineering/verification_store.py provides insert(tenant_id, memory_id, procedure_id, command, command_type, outcome, details, turn_distance) and query_by_memory(tenant_id, memory_id, limit) and query_by_procedure(tenant_id, procedure_id, limit) — all using the async pool from app lifespan

core-api tests: insert a verification run, query by memory, query by procedure; assert memory CASCADE delete removes the verification runs; assert cross-tenant isolation (tenant A cannot see tenant B's runs)
Migration runs cleanly against an empty DB and against the current migration chain (run: alembic upgrade head twice — second run is no-op)

reasons: Purely additive: new table + new service, no changes to existing tables or columns
FK ON DELETE CASCADE to memories ensures cleanup follows the existing memory delete path with no new logic
Rollback: drop the table via a down migration; zero impact on existing functionality

model_after: core-api/src/core_api/alembic/versions/ (latest migration — follow the numbering after the last existing migration)
common/database/postgres_client.py (for pool access pattern and async engine usage)
facts: memories table has id (uuid PK), tenant_id; verification_runs.tenant_id should match memories.tenant_id for row-level tenant isolation
procedure_id on verification_runs is not FK'd to the procedures table — procedures are identified by memory_id + metadata in practice across this codebase
Details column is jsonb — flexible for outcome payloads that differ per command_type (http status+body, bash stdout+stderr, fs found/size)
validation_commands: cd core-api && alembic upgrade head && alembic current

workItems.objective: Add memclaw_verify_run MCP tool: takes a procedure_id + command + command_type, spawns an isolated sub-agent in the current harness, executes the verification (bash command, HTTP assertion, or filesystem check), and records outcome + details to verification_runs. Success increments the procedure's success_count, failure increments failure_count — directly feeding the existing reliability ranker. Designed as the Isolated Verification Substrate that Loop Engineering Idea 3 requires.
acceptanceCriteria: core-api/src/core_api/tools/memclaw_verify_run.py defines a ToolSpec with command, command_type (bash|http|filesystem), procedure_id (required for reliability feedback), optional memory_id; tool registered via register() + mcp_register() and appears in the MCP tool list and scripts/export_tool_specs.py output

Execution engine dispatches by command_type: bash runs via subprocess in a sandboxed working directory (read-only mount, no network beyond localhost, timeout ≤ 30s); http makes a single GET/POST and asserts status_code + optional body substring; filesystem stats the path and asserts existence + optional size/content match — NO unrestricted code execution

On completion (success, fail, or error), insert into verification_runs via the LE-01 service and update the associated procedure_stats: success -> increment success_count, fail -> increment failure_count (reuses compute_reliability pathway from core-api/src/core_api/services/procedure_service.py)
trust_required >= 2 (mutations procedure reliability); auth check follows the _get_tenant/_get_agent_id pattern from existing tools

End-to-end test: register a procedure via memclaw_procedure_record -> memclaw_verify_run(command_type='bash', command='echo ok') -> assert outcome=pass, success_count incremented; memclaw_verify_run(command='exit 1') -> assert outcome=fail, failure_count incremented; procedure suggest reflects updated reliability
Sandboxed execution: test that a command attempting network access beyond localhost is blocked and outcome=error is recorded

reasons: Subprocess execution is the highest-trust surface in MemClaw — the sandbox must be airtight (no network, no privileged paths, strict timeout)
Failure in sandbox isolation could allow an untrusted command to affect the host environment
Rollback: remove the tool module + handler; verification_runs rows are inert historical data

security_notes: Sandbox model: subprocess.run with timeout=30, cwd set to a temp directory, environment stripped to a minimum (no credentials, no API keys), no network namespace (or deny-list firewall) — implementation detail chosen at build time based on what the deployment platform supports
http command_type: only single-request assertions, no persistent connections; timeout enforced
The tool is trust>=2 AND requires explicit procedure_id — the agent cannot run arbitrary commands without attributing them to a known procedure

workItems.objective: Add turn-index tracking to the memory write and recall paths, and implement the Turn-Distance Cost Factor (TDCF) computation. Turn index is a monotonically increasing conversation-scoped counter passed by the caller (the agent harness supplies it on each memclaw_write/memclaw_recall); TDCF = base_cost × 2^(turn_distance/10) where turn_distance = T_current − T_introduced. This gives agents a concrete cost signal for how stale a piece of context is.
acceptanceCriteria: common/models/memory.py gains turn_index (integer NULL — NULL for memories written before LE-03 or when the caller does not supply one; populated on write when caller passes it)

core-api write path (memclaw_write handler in mcp_server.py) accepts an optional turn_index (integer) in the write payload and persists it to the memory's turn_index column; the storage layer and write pipeline (common/pipelines/memory_write_pipeline.py) propagate it without truncation

core-api/src/core_api/services/loop_engineering/turn_distance.py provides compute_tdcf(turn_distance, base_cost=1.0) -> float using the formula base_cost × 2^(turn_distance/10); and compute_turn_distance(t_introduced, t_current) -> integer where either can be None (returning 0 when turn_index is absent — conservative assumption: fresh context)

Unit tests: TDCF(0) == 1.0 (no cost amplification for current-turn context), TDCF(10) == 2.0, TDCF(20) == 4.0, TDCF(None/absent) == 1.0 (conservative default); turn_distance computation: current=50, introduced=30 -> 20
Migration adds turn_index (integer, nullable, no default — only populated when caller provides it) to the memories table; index on (tenant_id, turn_index) for range queries

reasons: Adds a column to the memories table (hot path) — must be nullable with no default to avoid a full table rewrite on large tenants
The write path change must not break existing callers that do not pass turn_index (nullable, backwards-compatible)
Rollback: drop the column via a down migration; TDCF service becomes unused and can be removed

model_after: common/models/memory.py (memory dataclass + ORM model — the turn_index column addition point)
common/pipelines/memory_write_pipeline.py (write pipeline — propagate turn_index from write payload to storage row)
core-api/src/core_api/mcp_server.py memclaw_write handler (accept optional turn_index from the caller)
facts: TDCF formula: base_cost × 2^(turn_distance/10) — exponential doubling every 10 turns of staleness
turn_index is caller-supplied (the agent harness counts its own turns); MemClaw does not try to auto-detect turn boundaries
When turn_index is NULL (pre-LE-03 memories or callers that don't supply it), TDCF defaults to 1.0 — conservative, no penalty for unknown age

workItems.objective: Add memclaw_cost_audit MCP tool: exposes Turn-Distance Cost Factor analysis for recall results and write decisions. For a given recall operation, returns each returned memory's TDCF (how stale it is), base cost (1.0), and the cheapest alternative (another memory covering the same entity/scope with a lower turn_distance). For write operations, flags writes where the new memory's turn_index is within N turns of an active memory on the same entity — predicting early supersession and the associated TDCF cliff. Read-only.
acceptanceCriteria: core-api/src/core_api/tools/memclaw_cost_audit.py defines a ToolSpec with op (recall | write_preview), optional query/entity filter parameters; tool registered via register() + mcp_register() and appears in the MCP tool list

op='recall' computes TDCF for the most recent recall_event's result set using the LE-03 turn_distance service; returns per-memory: memory_id, turn_index, turn_distance, tdcf, and cheapest_alternative (another active memory on the same entity with lower turn_distance, or null if none); scoped to caller's tenant

op='write_preview' takes a proposed write (entity, scope, content) and checks for active memories on the same entity; returns the projected TDCF cliff if the new write supersedes a young existing memory (turn_distance < threshold, default 5) — a 'stale context warning' signal for the agent
trust_required >= 0 (read-only); tenant-scoped visibility rules match memclaw_list

Test: seed 3 memories on the same entity with turn_indices 10, 20, 40 (current turn = 50); recall -> cost_audit shows the turn_index=10 memory with TDCF=16.0 and cheapest_alternative=turn_index=40; write_preview shows no cliff when no entity collision, shows cliff warning when writing to a young existing entity
When turn_index is NULL on a memory, TDCF defaults to 1.0 and cheapest_alternative is null — the audit is conservative, not speculative

reasons: Read-only tool — no mutations to memories or procedures
Reuses existing visibility-scoped query paths and the LE-03 computation service
Rollback: remove the tool module; no schema or data change

facts: cheapest_alternative is defined as: another active memory on the same entity (matched via existing entity resolution) with the lowest turn_distance among memories with turn_distance < the current one
write_preview does NOT actually write — it is a dry-run signal for agents to evaluate cost before committing a write
TDCF cliff threshold default = 5 turns — configurable via organization_settings if needed later
validation_commands: pytest core-api/tests -k 'cost_audit or tdcf or turn_distance' -q

workItems.objective: Add memclaw_schedule_loop MCP tool: enables proactive loop propulsion — agents (or humans) can schedule future loop re-entry by creating propulsion tickets. Tickets are stored as documents in a reserved __loop_propulsion__ collection (reuses the existing memclaw_doc store, no new migration). A lifecycle cron handler (new forge-distill-style cron entry) runs on a fixed interval, reads active tickets, evaluates their trigger conditions, and fires matching tickets by invoking the task_payload (a structured loop instruction). Closes the scheduling half of 5-Move cycle Move 5 (propulsion).
acceptanceCriteria: Propulsion tickets stored via the existing document store in the reserved collection '__loop_propulsion__'; each ticket has: ticket_id (doc_id), title, trigger_type (delay|cron|webhook), trigger_at (timestamp for delay, cron expression for cron, null for webhook), task_payload (structured loop instruction — what the agent should do when the ticket fires), created_at, status (active|fired|cancelled), fired_at (null until fired)

core-api/src/core_api/tools/memclaw_schedule_loop.py defines a ToolSpec with ops: schedule (title, trigger_type, trigger_at/cron_expr, task_payload), cancel (ticket_id), list (status filter, limit); trust_required >= 2; appears in the MCP tool list

op='schedule' with trigger_type='delay' creates a ticket with trigger_at = now + delay_seconds; trigger_type='cron' stores the cron expression + next_fire_time (computed server-side); returns the ticket_id immediately

core-api/src/core_api/services/loop_engineering/propulsion_worker.py: invoked by lifecycle cron (new entry in organization_settings.security_audit_schedule_cron or a dedicated propulsion_schedule_cron), reads active tickets, evaluates triggers: delay tickets fire when now() >= trigger_at, cron tickets fire at their next_fire_time; fired tickets get status='fired', fired_at=now(); the worker calls the registered callback hook with the task_payload (implementation: invokes a configured loop entry endpoint or emits a structured event for the agent harness to consume)
op='cancel' sets status='cancelled' on a ticket by ticket_id; cancelled tickets are never fired by the worker

Test: schedule a delay ticket with trigger_at in the past -> run the worker -> assert ticket is fired and outcome logged; schedule a cron ticket -> advance time past next_fire_time -> run worker -> assert fired; cancel a ticket -> run worker -> assert NOT fired; cross-tenant: tenant A's worker does not fire tenant B's tickets
Trust gate: trust>=2 for schedule/cancel; list respects caller's visibility scope (same as memclaw_doc); ticket status transitions are auditable (fired_at is immutable once set)

reasons: Introduces a new cron-driven execution path — the worker loop must be idempotent (a fired ticket must not fire twice) and tenant-isolated
The task_payload is an opaque instruction to the agent harness — MemClaw stores and fires it, does not interpret or execute the payload directly (the harness is responsible for consumption)
Rollback: remove the tool + worker; __loop_propulsion__ documents are inert data

model_after: core-api/src/core_api/tools/memclaw_doc.py (op-dispatched document tool — same reservation pattern as env truths)
core-api/src/core_api/services/lifecycle_audit.py (cron handler registration pattern — lifecycle operations fan out via admin endpoint)
docs/operator-forge-cron.md (forge-distill cron pattern — the propulsion worker follows the same scheduler -> fanout -> per-tenant event model)
facts: Propulsion tickets use the document store (__loop_propulsion__ reserved collection) — no new alembic migration, no new table; same as env truths pattern
The worker does NOT execute the task_payload — it marks the ticket as fired and emits an event; the agent harness (Claude Code, OpenClaw loop, etc.) consumes the event and re-enters the loop
Cron trigger evaluation uses a standard cron library — next_fire_time is computed at schedule time and re-evaluated after each fire for recurring tickets
Follow the forge-distill cron pattern: external scheduler -> POST /admin/lifecycle/fanout/propulsion-fire -> per-tenant events -> in-process worker

workItems.objective: Write SPRINT-LOOP-ENGINEERING_SIGNOFF.md recording task outcomes, file changes for all 3 upgrades (verification subsystem, TDCF exposure, propulsion scheduling), verification evidence for each tool, and a Loop Engineering coverage table mapping each of the 5 load-bearing ideas to the MemClaw capabilities that now support them — proving 100% enablement post-sprint.
acceptanceCriteria: File exists at sprints/sprint-loop-engineering/SPRINT-LOOP-ENGINEERING_SIGNOFF.md
Task status table covers LE-01..LE-05 with DONE/PARTIAL/PENDING/BLOCKED

Verification section includes: pytest output for each tool (verify_run, cost_audit, schedule_loop); end-to-end evidence for the Isolated Verification path (LE-02: procedure_record → verify_run → suggest reflects updated reliability); TDCF correctness evidence (LE-03 unit tests + LE-04: seed memories at different turn_indices → audit shows correct TDCF values); propulsion firing evidence (LE-05: schedule delay ticket → worker fires it → ticket is in fired state)

Loop Engineering Coverage Table maps each of the 5 ideas to specific MemClaw capabilities: (1) Layer 4 Substrate -> existing memclaw_write/recall/evolve/lifecycle; (2) 5 Moves -> recall(1), write(2), verify_run(3), evolve(4), schedule_loop(5); (3) Isolated Verification -> verify_run + verification_runs; (4) Turn-Distance Cost -> turn_index + cost_audit + TDCF; (5) Amplifier -> memclaw_recall ranker (BM25+vector+entity crosslinks+recency+feedback) + verify_run reliability updates
Sprint close summary states whether 100% Loop Engineering enablement was achieved and lists any carried-forward items

implementation_notes: Include: task status table, changes-by-file, verification output per tool, Loop Engineering coverage table (5 ideas × pre/post capability mapping), carried-forward table, sprint close summary

warnings: LE-02 (memclaw_verify_run) is the highest-risk item — sandbox isolation for subprocess execution must be validated before merging. The security model is: trust>=2 + procedure_id required + no network + timeout + minimal environment. Do NOT loosen any of these constraints.

LE-01 and LE-03 each add an alembic migration (new table + new column). Run them in order: LE-01 first (new table has no dependencies), then LE-03 (adds turn_index to memories table). They do not conflict but share the migration chain.

LE-04 (cost_audit) is read-only and can be built and tested independently against the LE-03 service, but the value proposition is maximal when turn_index is populated — early testing will use NULL turn_index data.

LE-05 (schedule_loop) reuses the document store (__loop_propulsion__ collection) without a new migration — same pattern as env truths (BP-03 of brain-parity sprint). Ensure the reserved collection name does not collide with user-created collections.

The propulsion worker (LE-05) does NOT execute the task_payload — it marks the ticket as fired and emits an event. The agent harness is responsible for consuming the event and re-entering the loop. This is by design — MemClaw is a substrate, not a scheduler/orchestrator.

parallelWorkstreams: LE-01 -> LE-02 is the isolation-verification spine (new table before the tool that writes to it)
LE-03 -> LE-04 is the TDCF spine (turn_index column before the audit tool that reads it)
LE-05 (propulsion scheduling) is fully independent — uses the document store, no migration dependency
LE-01/02 and LE-03/04 tracks are independent of each other and can run concurrently
LE-Z01 signoff depends on all of LE-01..LE-05

loopEngineeringCoverage.idea_1_layer4_substratum: SUPPORTED (pre-sprint) — memclaw_write, recall, entity_get, evolve, lifecycle automation
loopEngineeringCoverage.idea_2_five_moves: FULLY SUPPORTED post-sprint — recall(1), write(2), verify_run(3), evolve(4), schedule_loop(5)
loopEngineeringCoverage.idea_3_isolated_verification: FULLY SUPPORTED post-sprint — verification_runs table + memclaw_verify_run isolated execution + reliability feedback
loopEngineeringCoverage.idea_4_turn_distance_cost: FULLY SUPPORTED post-sprint — turn_index on memories + TDCF computation + memclaw_cost_audit
loopEngineeringCoverage.idea_5_amplifier_of_judgment: SUPPORTED (pre-sprint + post-sprint reinforcement) — ranker (BM25+vector+entity crosslinks+recency+feedback) + procedure reliability updated by verify_run outcomes

deferredToNextSprint: Webhook trigger type for propulsion tickets (LE-05 ships delay+cron; webhook requires external event source integration)
TDCF surfaced in the recall ranker as a ranking signal (this sprint exposes it via cost_audit for the agent to act on; auto-weighting by TDCF in the ranker is a future optimization)
Cross-agent verification (verify_run executed by a separate verifier-agent harness rather than an inline subprocess — requires a multi-agent orchestration layer not present in MemClaw)
